coredata.dc:title: Influence of carbon nanodots on the Carbonate/CO2/Brine wettability and CO2-Brine interfacial Tension: Implications for CO2 geo-storage
coredata.dc:creator: Sakthivel, Sivabalan
Yekeen, Nurudeen
Theravalappil, Rajesh
Al-Yaseri, Ahmed
coredata.dc:description:
Understanding the interfacial and wettability phenomena of the CO2-brine and rock-CO2-brine system plays a key role in the geo-storage of CO2 and in their security containment. Carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been identified as a promising surface-active agent for altering the wettability from strongly oil-wet into water-wet in the carbonate reservoirs, thus it contributed for the oil recovery increment in the carbonate formations. However, no consideration was given to the application of CNDs for the enhancement of CO2 geological storage and containment security in the carbonate formations. In the current study, we assessed the impact of CNDs on the residual and structural trapping of CO2 in the carbonate reservoir. The contact angles (
θ
) of the CO2-brine-carbonate rock system were studied before and after the treatment of CNDs for both the clean and crude oil-aged samples. Subsequently, we also investigated the CO2-seawater interfacial tensions (IFT) with and without CNDs addition. All these measurements were performed as a function of CNDs concentration (0–1000 ppm), temperature (20–80 °C) and pressure (14.7–3000 psi). The results showed that the clean carbonate rock remained strongly water-wet before and after CNDs-treatment. The oil-wet carbonate sample was turned into CO2-wet at all investigated temperatures when the pressures increased to 1000 psi, however, it turns to be weakly water-wet upon treatment with CNDs at the highest pressure (3000 psi). Specifically, at 3000 psia and 20 °C, the contact angle of oil-wet carbonate reduced from 122° to 86° with the addition of CNDs at the increased concentration of 1000 ppm. Such wettability modifications in carbonate formations will enhance the efficacy of CO2 storage potential, and eventually, this will help to de-risk their security containment. The
θ
generally demonstrated a declining trend with the increase of CNDs concentration but it slightly increased with the rise in temperature and pressure. These results suggest that warmer reservoirs and increased storage depth could be unfavorable for CO2 storage in carbonate formations. Thus, the pre-injection of a minimal concentration of CNDs in the formation would improve CO2 storage effectively. Also, no significant change was observed in the CO2-seawater IFT in the presence of CNDs, which indicates that the capillary trapping of CO2 could be favorable due to their lower contact angle and high CO2-brine IFT as the most suitable condition to maintain the high capillarity. The study demonstrates that the highly stable, freely soluble, easy detectable, scalable, and economically viable CNDs has the potency to enhance the containment security of CO2 in carbonate formation.

head.ce:title: Influence of carbon nanodots on the Carbonate/CO2/Brine wettability and CO2-Brine interfacial Tension: Implications for CO2 geo-storage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Understanding the interfacial and wettability phenomena of the CO2-brine and rock-CO2-brine system plays a key role in the geo-storage of CO2 and in their security containment. Carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been identified as a promising surface-active agent for altering the wettability from strongly oil-wet into water-wet in the carbonate reservoirs, thus it contributed for the oil recovery increment in the carbonate formations. However, no consideration was given to the application of CNDs for the enhancement of CO2 geological storage and containment security in the carbonate formations. In the current study, we assessed the impact of CNDs on the residual and structural trapping of CO2 in the carbonate reservoir. The contact angles (
θ
) of the CO2-brine-carbonate rock system were studied before and after the treatment of CNDs for both the clean and crude oil-aged samples. Subsequently, we also investigated the CO2-seawater interfacial tensions (IFT) with and without CNDs addition. All these measurements were performed as a function of CNDs concentration (0–1000 ppm), temperature (20–80 °C) and pressure (14.7–3000 psi). The results showed that the clean carbonate rock remained strongly water-wet before and after CNDs-treatment. The oil-wet carbonate sample was turned into CO2-wet at all investigated temperatures when the pressures increased to 1000 psi, however, it turns to be weakly water-wet upon treatment with CNDs at the highest pressure (3000 psi). Specifically, at 3000 psia and 20 °C, the contact angle of oil-wet carbonate reduced from 122° to 86° with the addition of CNDs at the increased concentration of 1000 ppm. Such wettability modifications in carbonate formations will enhance the efficacy of CO2 storage potential, and eventually, this will help to de-risk their security containment. The
θ
generally demonstrated a declining trend with the increase of CNDs concentration but it slightly increased with the rise in temperature and pressure. These results suggest that warmer reservoirs and increased storage depth could be unfavorable for CO2 storage in carbonate formations. Thus, the pre-injection of a minimal concentration of CNDs in the formation would improve CO2 storage effectively. Also, no significant change was observed in the CO2-seawater IFT in the presence of CNDs, which indicates that the capillary trapping of CO2 could be favorable due to their lower contact angle and high CO2-brine IFT as the most suitable condition to maintain the high capillarity. The study demonstrates that the highly stable, freely soluble, easy detectable, scalable, and economically viable CNDs has the potency to enhance the containment security of CO2 in carbonate formation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Many nations of the world are presently aiming at achieving the CO2-free economy and net zero carbon emissions by 2050 [1–3]. To achieve the decarbonization objectives, it is essential to either stop the global reliance on fossil fuel or to ensure that the amount of greenhouse gas (mainly CO2) that is produced from fossil burning and other anthropogenic sources, is the same as the amount that is removed from the atmosphere through carbon capture, utilization, and storage (CCUS). CCUS is a more promising option since it will take more time to reach out to the non-reliant on fossil fuels due to the increasing global demand for energy occasion by the growing world population and rapid rate of industrialization [4–5]. In general, the CCUS can be achieved by injecting the CO2 into the depleted oil reservoirs; as well in the deep saline formations and aquifers [6–9].

Understanding the interaction between the injected CO2 and the host reservoir rock, as well as with the resident brine plays a huge role for both the enhanced oil recovery and in the CO2 geo-storage, which is so crucial determinant factor for keeping the CO2 permanently stored in storage formations [10–18]. Particularly, the knowledge of the interfacial phenomena, such as oil–water and/or gas–water interfacial tensions and the rock-oil–water and/or rock-gas–water wettabilities are the key factors for both the successful oil recovery [19–22] and thus for the geo-storage of CO2 in the deep reservoirs [9–18,23]. Wettability is an interfacial property that depicts the propensity of a particular fluid to favorably wet a surface in the presence of another immiscible fluid, whereas the IFT between two liquids is the free energy per unit surface area required to create that interface [24]. However, both the IFT and wettabilities are so crucial and interconnected parameters that govern the flow of fluids and its distribution in the porous media. Further, they also influence on the capillarity and relative permeability of the reservoirs [25–28]. The successful IFT and wettability modifications would result in positive outcomes for both the initial and residual CO2 saturations; as well, the brine distributions in porous media.

In the reservoir, the injected CO2 can remains be immobilized by various mechanisms, such as, structural trapping, capillary trapping, mineralization trapping, and solubility trapping. Structural trapping of CO2 in the caprock is so essential for preventing gas leakages through the overlying seal [9–18,23], where the CO2 injection was to be employed under pressure. However, due to the presence of the pre-occupied denser fluid (resident brine), the injected CO2 would tend to moves upward and in lateral directions as a response to buoyancy force and formation fluid pressures, whereby CO2 tends to accumulate in stratigraphic traps under seal (caprock) [29–31]. Capillary trapping (also known as residual trapping) takes place when CO2 is injected under pressure at deep reservoirs in the presence of resident brines. It is a major mechanism in the geo-storage of the CO2, it occurs during the brine imbibition. The formation brine imbibition takes place to re-occupy the pore spaces that are previously occupied by the CO2 [32]. Subsequently, brine fluids would tend to moves downward and CO2 would moves upward during the imbibition process as the capillary and buoyancy forces act at pore surfaces and in the fluids, correspondingly. In which, a significant amount of CO2 gets trapped as an immobile phase into the pore spaces [32–36]. The solubility trapping cannot be considered as the permanent solution as it is subjected for a reversible process upon varying the pressure and temperature of the system. The contribution of mineralization was observed to be very minimal due to their extremely slower kinetics.

About 60% of the world’s reservoirs are carbonate formation, they are mostly complex, reactive, heterogenous, oil-wet (hydrophobic and CO2-wet) formations, rarely in some cases they were identified as intermediate-wet [37–38]. So, the security of CO2 geo-storage in carbonate formations needs more attention. The capillary pressure and diffusion of stored CO2 gas across the caprocks depends on rock wettability
( θ )
and the rock-brine-CO2 interfacial interaction
( γ )
as shown in Equation (1) [18,39–40].

where
P c
is capillary pressure,

denotes the contact angle (°) which is usually measured through the denser phase (through brine in this study),
γ
is CO2-brine IFT (mN/m), and r represents the capillary radius (m). At CO2-wet conditions, the leakages and upwelling of the buoyant carbon dioxide across the caprock will be high because the buoyancy pressure of the gas plume is higher than the capillary force. Lower contact angle (water-wet condition) and high CO2/brine IFT would ensure that the threshold capillary pressure is not surpassed [18].

The study of injecting surface-active agents such as surfactants, smart water, polymers, and nanofluids into the oil-wet formation is the most commonly employed procedure in the literature. That helps to alters their original wettability and influences the mobilization of the trapped oil and improves the overall oil displacements. For instance, Bayat et al. [41] demonstrated a significant reduction in the wettability of the oil-wet carbonate outcrop using 0.005 wt/v% of the nanofluids, such as SiO2, TiO2, and Al2O3. The particle sizes of them were ranged between 20 and 40 nm. They also reported that they were synergistically reduced the surface tension from 26.5 mN/m to 17 mN/m when there is a brine medium (3 wt% NaCl). Mohammadi et al. [42] was also demonstrated the similar wettability modifications in the carbonate sample using 0.5 wt% of Al2O3 nanofluids.

AlZaabi et al. [43] investigated the effect of CTAB and SDBS surfactants for the wettability study on the of pure calcite and three various carbonate samples. It was reported that the calcite surface turns to be more of hydrophilic (water-wet) in the presence of CTAB, but a higher concentration of the surfactant did not yield the same. In which, the contact angle values were observed to be decreased with the increase in temperature (303–323 K) and slightly increased with the increase in pressure (0.1–20 MPa). They also found that the increase of calcite content in the rock sample reduces the contact angle more significantly. Jafarbeigi et al. [44] reported the effect of nanocomposites (UiO-66-NH2/TiO2 nanofluid-UT) on oil–water IFT, carbonate wettability (contact angle), and core-flooding. They observed a significant decrease in IFT from 22 to 3.3 mN/m, as well a reduction of contact angles was observed from 118°to 37°. Ramezani et al. [45] investigated the hybrid nature of nanofluids and surfactants, where they found that the presence of SiO2 nanoparticles in sodium lauryl sulfate solution reduced the IFT from 0.36 mN/m to 0.1 mN/m; as well the contact angles reduced from 49° to 32.5°.

A similar approach can also be extended for CO2 geo-storage, where the reservoirs can be pre-treated with suitable chemicals to alter the reservoir’s wettability, and thus the storage capacity/efficiency can be boosted more significantly. The analog studies were demonstrated in the recent literatures using different chemicals that can alters the reservoir/caprocks wettability. Such treatment of rock samples with chemicals (surfactants, nanofluids, etc) were in fact altered the rock's wettability originally from oil-wet into more towards the water-wet condition and thus it facilitated for more CO2 storage by increasing their residual and structural trapping.

Ali et al. [46–47] evaluated the influence of SiO2 and Al2O3 nanofluids on the wettability of organic-acid-aged quartz and mica respectively, they observed a significant reduction of the contact angles upon treating the sample with the nanofluids, asserting that the rock surface wettability was modified from CO2-wet to water-wet due to the adsorption of the nanofluids on the aged rock surfaces. Similarly, Al-Yaseri et al [48] and Ali et. al [13] reported the modification of Basalt surface into hydrophilic state due to SiO2 nanofluids treatment, as a result, it improved the CO2 column height more significantly. Recently, Arain et al. [14] investigated the efficacy of Al2O3 (13–50 nm) nanofluids dispersed in 10 wt% of NaCl for the study of wettability modification in the carbonate samples, where they observed that treating the reservoir with the optimal concentration of nanofluids was successfully altered the wettability from CO2-wet into water-wet, which indeed improves the storage capacity as well the security containment of the reservoir. Yekeen et al. [18] observed that the surface of Marcellus shale, with high total organic carbon (14.8%) shifted more into water-wet when the sample was saturated with nanoparticle-surfactant solution prepared from rice husk nanosilica and saponin. They also noted higher CO2-brine IFT than the CO2-nanoparticle and surfactant hybrid IFT systems. Table 1 shows the summary of key literatures that reported the effect of various nanofluids and surfactants on the measurement of CO2-brine IFT and rock-CO2-brine wettability.

However, most of the previously studied chemicals like nanofluids and surfactants are limited to laboratory-scale experiments. Field pilot of such technologies for the integrated application of the successful EOR and CO2 geo-storage is rarely reported due to their lack of upscaling technology. The scalability of such materials with their suitable functionality is so challenging at the larger scale, and it is so important to withstand them at high temperature (150° C) and high salinity (240 kppm) subsurface conditions [19]. Also, most of the conventional chemicals like the nanoparticles, surfactants, polymers, and biomolecules tend to degrade and agglomerate in the reservoir condition, particularly in the harsh saline environment, which results in for the formation damages by plugging the pore throat and the reduction of permeability. This, in fact, yields an unfavorable environment for their possible subsurface storage applications [19]. For the effective CO2 storage, we need a candidate which is highly stable and effective wettability modifier at reservoir conditions. The chemical treatments should reduce the contact angle (by altering the wettability), as well as increase the CO2-brine IFT, to improve the storage potential more effectively. However, as shown in Table 1, the application of all nanofluids and surfactants that are reported in literature so far, they tend decrease both the CO2-brine IFT and rock-CO2-brine contact angles.

Yet, the researchers have not succeeded to find the chemicals that can shift the rock wettability more towards water-wet; as well as to increase the CO2-brine IFT. As seen in Table 1, SiO2 and Al2O3 were the most studied nanoparticles in these applications. Few recent studies had witnessed that the use of carbon-based nanofluids could be the most effective surface-active agent to perform the required wettability and IFT modifications in the subsurface condition. Yekeen et al. [49] and Okunade et al. [50] reported the applications of multi-walled carbon nanofluids on the rock-wetting phenomenon as well as oil-brine IFT. Their results showed that the drop in contact angles and oil-brine IFT was much more significant in the presence of multi-walled carbon nanofluids compared to SiO2, Al2O3, and TiO2 nanofluids. This effect was attributed to the effective orientation of multi-walled carbon nanotubes at the liquid–liquid and solid–liquid interface due to their cylindrical shapes, however, they are not cost-effective, and they cannot be considered as a viable option for field applications.

Kanj et al. [19] reported the interesting application of low-cost organic carbon nanodots, (CNDs) for alteration of the wettability in the carbonate reservoir and it was observed that the wettability of the oil-wet carbonate sample was dropped by about 50% when the rock was treated with just 200 ppm of CNDs concentration. They are extremely tiny particles in the range of 3–8 nm, and they had shown a high stability even in the harsh reservoir environment of high temperature (150 °C) and high salinity (240 kppm) conditions. Also, it was synthesized in a one-pot synthesis using the most viable citric acid and ethanolamine. They are highly fluorescent, detectable, soluble, and economically viable materials. It was composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen. As well, they were upscaled for a larger quantity (more than 1000 kg) and tested for the field trial for tracer applications. However, the effects of CNDs on the wettability of rock-CO2-brine systems as well as CO2-brine IFT was not investigated to assess their potential application on improving the geo-storage of CO2.

The crude oil sample used in this study was obtained from one of the Saudi Arabian oil reservoirs, it consists more of: saturates (36.2%) and aromatics (50%), resins (11%), as well as asphaltenes (2.8%) [57]. The physicochemical properties of the crude oil sample are shown in Table 2 .

The Carbon nanodots (CNDs) was synthesized in a simple one-pot synthesis using the citric acid and ethanolamine as the precursors. Later, they were purified with the dialysis method [58–61]. Moreover, the CNDs are highly fluorescent and easily detectable even at extremely lower concentrations like 1 ppm. They were demonstrated for stability, mobility, and retention studies at the reservoir conditions [61], and found to be highly stable and mobile even at high temperature (423 K) and high saline (240 kppm) conditions.

In general, Saudi Arabian reservoirs are one of high saline formation, which consists of the total dissolved solids (TDS) for about 2–5 M with the complex ions formulation that contains both the mono- and divalent ions. In such a reservoir, the injection seawater (SW) used to be employed is about 1.2 M salty. To mimic such conditions in this study we used the same saline formulation. The detail of the ionic composition is tabulated in Table 3
.

Subsequently, we prepared several pieces of rock chips (about 40 pieces) with the dimension of 1″ diameter and 3 mm thickness using the Indiana limestone outcrop. All these samples were prepared by using the same outcrop plug (It is more of homogeneous), the petrophysical properties of all the rock chips are considered to be uniform for the chip samples that were prepared for this study. After preparing the rock slices, the surface of the samples was polished thoroughly using the sandpapers and cleaned using the Soxhlet extraction apparatus, and dried in the oven for 1 day at 100 °C. After that, we ran the XRD mineral analysis of a few samples and presented the results only one sample in Fig. 1 and Table 4 for the sake of simplicity. It can be understood from both Fig. 1 and Table 4, that the sample is more abundant in calcium carbonate. Accordingly, we also measure the surface roughness of these samples using Surface roughness Analyzer (KRÜSS, THORLABS MB 1530F/M) and found the root mean square height of 0.04595 mm (Fig. 2
a). The three-dimensional view of the clean carbonate surface also showed that it is relatively more of clean calcite as shown in Fig. 2 a.

In parallel, we also acquired the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images for the clean, oil-wet, and CNDs-treated rock chips (Fig. 3 ). Compared to the clean rock sample (Fig. 3 a), the SEM image acquired after aging the rock sample in crude oil showed the spread of oil droplets on the rock surface which is responsible for the hydrophobicity of the crude oil-aged carbonate sample (Fig. 3 b). Also, the de-adsorption of oil can be clearly seen upon treating the oil-wet sample with CNDs suspension (Fig. 3 c), suggesting that the modification of the surface with the CNDs was responsible for reversal of crude oil-aged calcite surface wettability from CO2 wet state to the weakly water-wet state.

Thereafter, one-fourth of the clean samples were vacuum saturated in seawater and aged for 1-month in the seawater at the ambient condition. Similarly, one-fourth of the samples were vacuum saturated in CNDs solution (at a specific concentration) and aged as well in the same CNDs solution for a 1-month time duration at the ambient condition. Later, the rest of the samples were vacuum saturated in crude oil and aged in an oven for 2 weeks at 100 °C, which turns the sample's wettability to more oil-wet characteristics to mimic the realistic condition of the Saudi reservoirs. Later, half of these oil-wet samples were aged in seawater and the remaining samples were aged in CNDs solution at the ambient condition for the same 1-month time duration. Later, all these four types of samples were studied for the wettability measurements as detailed below.

In this study, we employed the Interfacial Tensiometer (IFT-700, Vinci Technologies) for measuring both the contact angle and the IFT. contact angle measurement was conducted with the sessile drop method and interfacial tension measurements was carried out through the pendant drop technique. This instrument consists of a rock holder, a syringe needle aligned straight to the rock holder to land a drop of water while dispensing from the top, a high-pressure cell with transparent sapphire glass, syringe pumps to pump the brine/nanofluids and CO2 fluids, thermostat for controlling the temperature, high-resolution camera to capture the images, and the software for image analysis.

Initially, a piece of rock chip sample was loaded in the rock holder and the cell was purged with the test fluid (CO2 gas) and followed by which the required test pressure and temperature of the system were set and left to stabilize for at least 1 h. Subsequently, the dispensing brine fluid (seawater) was also loaded into the syringe needle. Once the system reaches the equilibrated temperature and pressure, brine droplet was dispensed from the top of the cell using the needle. It slowly landed on the rock surface. The drop was allowed some time to stabilize, and the rock-CO2-water contact angle was measured through the water phase. Furthermore, we also measure the CO2-water IFT as a function of pressure and temperature using the same device. Experiments were conducted with and without CNDs. In this, a drop brine or CNDs fluid was suspended in the CO2 medium, and their corresponding interfacial forces were measured.

As mentioned earlier, in this we investigated the wettability of the rock-CO2-brine system for both the clean (oil-free) and oil-wet samples as a function of temperature, pressure, and additives (CNDs) concentration. Figs. 4 and 5
shows the wettability assessment of both the clean and oil-wet samples as a function of temperature and pressure with no addition of CNDs. As expected, it is clear that the oil-wet samples are identified to be more CO2-wet than the clean samples as the contact angles of the oil-wet samples are much higher than the clean ones. As more oil invades the pore spaces of the rock with increasing saturation time, the layer of water on the surface of the rock grows thinner until it finally collapses, and that in fact renders the direct contact between the oil and the rock surface. How strongly the oil adheres to the rock is a function of the wetting preference of the rock and the crude oil composition. Overall, the phenomenon that we observed for the oil-wet carbonate samples is more of hydrophobic.

Precisely, at 80 °C, the contact angle of the pure carbonate increased from 13° to 96° when the rock was aged in crude oil at ambient pressure (14.7 psia). Similarly, while increasing the pressure of the system from ambient to 3000 psia, the contact angle was observed to be raised from 42° to 138°. These results indicate that the wetting state of the rock surface shifted from strongly water wet to CO2-wet when the rock was aged in crude oil. It is also obvious from the plotted graphs that the increase of temperature and pressure increases the contact angle, which indicates the effectiveness of crude oil in making the surface to be more hydrophobic at higher pressure and temperature. In which, the residual trapping capacities of the carbonate reservoir is expected to be reduced significantly in the presence of crude oil and/or organic contaminations inherent in geo-storage formations [62–65].

Albeit high temperature and pressure have a significant impact on the wettability for both the clean and oil-wet samples, the clean carbonate remained at strongly water wet (measure contact angle is 42°, please refer Fig. S1 for wettability images) even at the extremely high temperature (80 °C) and high pressure (3000 psia). However, the wettability of the oil-wet sample is transformed from weakly water-wet (
θ =
73°) to fully CO2-wet state (
θ =
103°) upon increasing the pressure from 14.7 to 1000 psia, at the room temperature (20 °C). Oil-wet carbonate demonstrated CO2-wet behaviors when the pressure reached 1000 psi at all experimented temperatures.

These results are in good agreement with the literature, where several authors reported that higher contact angle values were determined at high pressure for calcite, quartz, basalt, mica, and shale [66–69]. Systematically, the density of CO2 increased with increasing pressure resulted in increasing molecular interaction and reduction in carbonate rock/CO2 cohesive energies with pressure. Thus, the reduction in CO2-calcite IFT at high pressure encouraged the receding (withdrawal) of the water from the carbonate surface (water de-wetting) and the spreading of CO2 on the portion initially occupied by the water [52]. Higher values of contact angles at high temperatures were reported for the wettability of quartz-CO2-brine systems in previous research. This phenomenon was attributed to the increasing tendency for the –OH bonds between silanol group of rock substrate, and H2O molecules of brine to be broken at elevated temperature, resulting in de-wetting of the rock surface, and increasing CO2 wettability [70–72].

Findings from this research generally showed that CO2 injection into crude oil-aged carbonate reservoirs or organic acid-contaminated reservoirs (such as depleted hydrocarbon carbonate reservoirs) at high temperature and pressure would likely lead to a considerable reduction in residual as well as structural trapping potential due to capillary leakage (diffusion of CO2 cross the caprock and storage rock). Accordingly, the oil-wet reservoirs such as depleted oil reservoirs seem to be potentially not a viable formation for the geo-storage of CO2. But the modification of wettability of such reservoirs into water-wet by CNDs would yield better storage potential.

Later, as shown in Figs. 6-7
, we investigated the effect of CNDs additives on the rock-CO2-brine wettability for both the clean and oil-wet samples as a function of temperature and pressure. As seen in Figs. 6-7, the addition CNDs reduced their contact angle more significantly for both the clean and oil-wet samples. As observed in Figs. 4 and 6, the addition of merely 100 ppm of CNDs reduced their contact angle about 40% (from 42 to 25°) at the extreme temperature (80 °C) and pressure (3000 psia). Overall, the sample remains to be strongly water-wet, but it is more with the addition of CNDs, also the CNDs are more effective at the extreme condition than the ambient condition. Likewise, we also observed 27% of drop in the contact angle for the oil-wet sample (refer Figs. 5 and 7) at the same experimental condition with 100 ppm of CNDs. It indicates that the injection CNDs prior to geo-storage of CO2 would indeed be of huge benefit at both the saline aquifer and in the depleted oil reservoirs, however, it will be more prominent in the saline aquifer due to their favorable wettability (strongly water-wet).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of CNDs concentration on the rock-CO2-brine wettability
ce:section.ce:para: In this, we investigated the effect of CNDs concentration in the oil-wet carbonate samples. As shown in Fig. 8 , it is clearly revealed that the CNDs treated rock CNDs treated rock demonstrated lower contact with increasing CNDs concentration from 10 ppm −1000 ppm, confirming that the adsorption of the CNDs on rock substrate shifted the surface wettability into more hydrophilic state. When the concentration of CNDs was increased, there was enough of the CNDs in the bulk solution that sufficiently spread on the rock surface and react with active sites of carbonate. Results of previous research has shown that low CO2 wettability of nanofluid-treated reservoir and caprock is due to the formation of monolayer and multi-layers hydrophilic clusters of the nanoparticles which tightly clung to the rock surfaces [18,48,52]. It confirms that the CNDs additives has the potency to alters the surface wettability more towards water wet as the CNDs are more of hydrophilic in nature. For instance, at ambient conditions, the contact angle of oil-wet carbonate sample was measured to be 73°, however when the pressure was increased to 3000 psia at the same temperature, their contact angle was shifted to 122° (refer Fig. 5). This indicates that the crude oil-aged carbonate was weakly water-wet at ambient pressure, whereas at the high pressure they behaved to be CO2-wet. Such high CO2-wet characteristics (high contact angle) could initiate an upwards concentrated force resulting in CO2 leakage across the caprock and failure of CO2 sequestration projects.

However, as depicted in Fig. 8, the addition of just 100 ppm of CNDs was able to shift the contact angle from 122 to 110° at the same temperature (20 °C) and pressure (3000 psia). Continuously, the increase of concentration upto 1000 ppm of CNDs had dropped the contact angle to 86° at the same experimental condition of 20 °C and in 3000 psia, which is about 30% reduction. While for the case of ambient condition (20 °C, 14.7 psia), the addition of CNDs at 1000 ppm had reduced their contact angle from 73 to 64°, which is merely about 12%. Therefore, the CNDs additives seems to be more effective at the elevated pressure rather the ambient condition. Thus, it is clear that the addition of CNDs had the capability to alter the oil-wet carbonate reservoir into less water-wet state at representative CO2 geo-storage conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of CNDs on the CO2-Seawater IFT
ce:section.ce:para:
Subsequently, the effect of CNDs on the CO2-Seawater IFT was also investigated as a function of CNDs concentration at 20 °C and in the broad ranges of pressure (14.7–3000 Psia), and their result was presented in Fig. 9 . It is worthy to note that the increase of CNDs concentration from 20 to 1000 ppm resulted in a very slight drop of IFT. As observed in Fig. 9, the incremental concentration of CNDs (from 20 to 1000 ppm) dropped the IFT to about 1.6 mN/m at the highest studied pressure of 3000 psia. This change in CO2-seawater IFT can be considered as very insignificant within the standard deviation of the IFT measurement (
±
2
m N / m )
. The insignificant change in IFT can be associated with the influence of CO2 adsorption on the seawater and CO2-seawater chemical interactions.

At the realistic geo-storage conditions, we expect no significant change in the CO2-seawater IFT in presence of CNDs because as CO2 is absorbed by water during CO2-seawater IFT measurement, series of chemical interactions/reactions will occur between the seawater and CO2 to form carbonic acid (H2CO3), a weak acid that will dissociate into H2CO3 – and increased concentration of hydrogen ions (H+ ions) in solution as shown in equation (2) and (3).
(2) CO2 (g) + H2O (l) ⇌ H2CO3 (aq)
(3) H2CO3 (aq) ⇋ HCO3 – (aq) + H+ (aq)

The seawater will become more acidic due to the increasing concentration of hydrogen ion in the bulk solutions, reducing the quantity of Ca2+ (carbonate ions from calcite) that is available for electrostatic interaction with negatively charged CNDs. Although the CNDs-treated carbonate surface can be acidified by the CO2 to release more calcium ion from the surface of the carbonate into the bulk solution to increase the positive charges on carbonate surface, the quantity of the Ca2+ available for the electrostatic interaction with CNDs will reduce in presence of H+ ion. Thus, there was no significant difference in CO2-seawater IFT and CO2-CNDs nanofluid IFT. Moreover, the zeta potential of CNDs in seawater was found to be positive (+39 mV), implying that there will be no significant interaction between CNDs and carbonate surface in the presence of seawater.

In the past, we demonstrated the CNDs for their colloidal stability, mobility, retention, detectability to ppm/ppb levels, easy and inexpensive synthesis, and scalability for a larger quantity (∼1000/kg). Later, we also investigated it for tracers application, as well as for the wettability alterations, and thus for enhanced oil recovery. However, the potential applications of CNDs for altering the wettability of the carbonate reservoir and CO2-seawater IFT for enhancing the CO2 containment safety in carbonate geo-storage formations has not been reported previously. Thus, in the current study, we investigated the contact angle and IFT of the rock-CO2-brine and CO2-seawater systems, respectively, to evaluate the impact of the CNDs treatment in the carbonate reservoir with the focus of assessing them for CO2 storage potentials. The main conclusions from the study are as follows:
1.
The clean carbonate sample is intrinsically water-wet at the highest investigated temperature (80 °C) and pressure (3000 psia) with contact angle of 42° and remained strongly water-wet (
θ =
25°) while treating the sample with the CNDs, which suggests that the CNDs influences the rock wettability to shift into more hydrophilic state at the typical geo-storage conditions.
2.
Oil-wet carbonate rocks were found to be CO2-wet from 1000 psia and at all investigated temperatures (20–––80 °C), indicating that the CO2 residual and structural trapping potentials of oil and organic acid contaminated carbonate formation (depleted hydrocarbon reservoirs) will be low. However, a considerable shift in wettability towards weakly-water wet state was observed due to CNDs-treatment suggesting that the potential risks for CO2 storage in depleted carbonate formations could reduce through prior treatment with CNDs nanofluids before CO2 injection.
3.
Contact angles increased with increasing temperature and pressure suggesting that warmer reservoirs and increasing CO2 storage depth could be unfavorable for CO2 storage in oil-wet carbonate because of the increasing acidity of calcite solution at the carbonate surface resulting in decreasing quantity of Ca2+ ions that will be potentially involved in electrostatic interaction with negatively charged CNDs.
4.
Generally, CNDs has no significant influence on CO2-seawater IFT as the IFT remains almost constant with increasing CNDs concentration because as CO2 is absorbed by seawater, the seawater becomes acidic resulting in increased concentration of hydrogen ions (H+ ions).
5.
Results of this study generally demonstrate that contact angle decreased, whereas CO2-seawater IFT remain unchanged in the presence of CNDs, which are favorable conditions for maintaining high capillary pressure. However, the carbonate reservoirs are quite heterogenous, and the carbonate mineral-CO2 reactions could also play a prominent role in CO2-storage in CND-treated rock.

Overall, the CNDs demonstrated a positive impact on the wettability modification of the oil-wet reservoirs. Thus, the pre-injection of diluted concentrations of easy scalable CNDs into the depleted oil reservoir would favor the vision for a successful CO2 storage and improved security containment by shifting the reservoir wettability more towards the water-wet. The results suggest that possibly this can be the most effective and viable additive to the pre-flush waterflood in the field application to prepare the reservoir for the improved CO2 storage potential.
